title: Leave It Alone
passage:
For as long as anyone at the leases can remember, the rule of a fine pearl has been a simple one: leave the oyster alone. A pearl is nacre laid down in patient layers around a seeded nucleus, and every disturbance — a panel hauled up to be scrubbed, a net turned over, a diver's shadow crossing the racks — is a jolt of stress, and stress, the old hands say, is a blemish waiting to happen. Keep the water steady, keep the handling to the barest minimum, and let the animal get on in peace with the one slow thing it knows how to do. It is a calm, humane story, and it has shaped the way most farmers work. What it quietly skips is that a pearl is not sold by how much nacre an oyster piles on, but by how evenly it lays that nacre down.

That gap was put to the test in a working bay. At the Alenga Bay pearl cooperative, a team led by the shellfish physiologist Renata Vasco followed six seasons of harvests across fourteen leases, holding the water temperature and the oyster strain steady while varying just one thing: whether a lease turned and cleaned its panels on a frequent cycle, unsettling the oysters again and again, or left them very largely alone. On the figure every farmer watches, the result came out exactly as the old rule promised. The undisturbed oysters laid down more nacre, and laid it faster, and they did so right across the bay — in the leases that fussed endlessly over their panels and in the leases that barely touched them, alike. Measured by the sheer thickness of the coat, calm was simply more pearl.

Then Vasco's team stopped weighing the nacre and began grading what a buyer actually pays for. A pearl's worth follows the evenness of its layers far more than their bulk: a thick coat laid on crookedly is a lumpy, baroque thing worth little, while a thinner coat laid down true is round, lustrous and dear. In the leases that gave their oysters a mild, regular disturbance — a gentle turning of the panels on a set cycle — the share of round, top-grade pearls climbed as the disturbance grew, but only up to a point. In the leases that left the oysters wholly in peace, grade rose slowly and then, once the coat passed a certain thickness, began to slip, because the nacre went on building lump upon lopsided lump. Each gentle unsettling, Vasco's team suspects, makes the oyster draw in and reseat its mantle squarely against the nucleus, so the layers that follow go on straight; left entirely undisturbed, the mantle drifts, and the coat thickens to one side. Past a certain threshold, though, disturbance stopped buying anything at all: turned too often, the oysters simply halted their work or died on the panel, and a dead oyster yields no pearl.

This is not how the trade usually talks. Tomas Broek, who runs the largest lease on the bay, will have none of the hedging. "Never touch a working oyster," he told me. "Every time you haul a panel you rob the animal of a week's growth, and for what? Leave them be and the pearls take care of themselves — they always have." Vasco is more careful. A measured disturbance can be built into the cycle, she agrees, but the window is narrow and easily overshot: too little does nothing, too much empties the panel, and the right dose is not the same for every oyster — the older animals, she found, bear more handling than the young.

The six-year record, read whole, is more divided than Broek's rule allows. It does not show that handling is free — the growth it costs is real, and past the threshold the losses are steep. What it shows is that "calm oyster, better pearl" holds only while the thing you are measuring is thickness; grade a harvest by roundness instead, and a measured disturbance, kept the right side of the threshold, buys an evenness that perfect stillness never does. The number that matters in the end is not how much nacre an oyster can pile on while nobody bothers it. It is how true the layers lie — and what keeps them true, the bay's own record suggests, is not being left utterly in peace but being unsettled, gently and on time.
— Iris Halloran

nacre = mother-of-pearl, the layered material an oyster secretes around a seed to build a pearl
mantle = the soft tissue whose outer surface lays down the nacre

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: That the surest way to grow a fine pearl is to handle the oyster as little as possible and let it lay its nacre down in perfect peace.
That calm oysters lay more nacre, but the pearls that sell are the round ones, and roundness comes from measured disturbance, not stillness.
That pearl farmers would do best to cull the oysters that produce lumpy, baroque coats and reseed the survivors with fresh nuclei.
That a pearl forms as an oyster wraps a seeded nucleus in patient layers of nacre until it is ready to be harvested.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text gist), edge position 1. WHY-KEY (B): the arc runs phenomenon (the received rule, 'leave the oyster alone ... keep the handling to the barest minimum') -> evidence (Vasco's six seasons across fourteen leases; 'The undisturbed oysters laid down more nacre, and laid it faster ... in the leases that fussed endlessly over their panels and in the leases that barely touched them, alike') -> complication (graded for roundness, the disturbed leases' 'share of round, top-grade pearls climbed as the disturbance grew', while the wholly-undisturbed leases' grade 'began to slip') -> verdict, paragraph 5: 'calm oyster, better pearl holds only while the thing you are measuring is thickness; grade a harvest by roundness instead, and a measured disturbance ... buys an evenness that perfect stillness never does.' B paraphrases that bounded verdict without lifting a distinctive run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope_error -- promotes the opening pitch ('leave the oyster alone') to the thesis; it is precisely the claim the passage sets out to qualify, not its point. C = surface_word_match / outside_knowledge -- recycles the 'lumpy, baroque' vocabulary into a cull-and-reseed policy the text never issues; the passage weighs disturbance against roundness but never tells farmers to cull or reseed. D = detail-as-main / wrong_location -- accurately reports the paragraph-1 mechanism of how a pearl forms ('nacre laid down in patient layers around a seeded nucleus') but that is scene-setting, not the whole-text focus; tempting because it is the passage's most concrete image.

questions.prompt: What does the text say about how much nacre the undisturbed oysters laid down compared with the frequently disturbed ones?

options.text: They laid down more nacre, and laid it faster, and did so in every lease alike, whether it fussed over its panels or barely touched them.
They laid down more nacre, and would have gone on adding thickness without any limit at all, however long the oysters were simply left completely alone.
They laid down more nacre only in the leases that were left alone, while the frequently disturbed leases saw no gain in thickness at all.
They laid down more nacre, and that thicker coat was also whiter and more lustrous than the coat the disturbed oysters built.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names the comparison (undisturbed versus frequently disturbed) but not the direction or scope of the result. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 2 states that the undisturbed oysters 'laid down more nacre, and laid it faster, and they did so right across the bay — in the leases that fussed endlessly over their panels and in the leases that barely touched them, alike.' A paraphrases both the fact (more nacre, faster) and its across-the-board scope. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = quantifier_upgrade (the engineered dominant trap) -- keeps the real fact but absolutizes it into thickness accruing 'without any limit at all', which the passage never claims and in fact undercuts, since past a certain thickness the coat's grade 'began to slip'. C = wrong_location / cross-arm swap -- the split between fussed-over and barely-touched leases governs the roundness result in paragraph 3, not the nacre thickness, which rose in both kinds of lease 'alike'; tempting because that split is the passage's central distinction. D = outside_knowledge -- adds that the thicker coat was 'whiter and more lustrous', a claim the passage never makes; the text ties lustre to evenness, not to thickness, and never says the calm oysters' coat was whiter.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what happened to the share of top-grade pearls in the leases that left their oysters wholly in peace?

options.text: It collapsed almost at once, so that the wholly undisturbed leases produced hardly any round, top-grade pearls at all.
It climbed higher the thicker the coat grew, reaching its very best in the leases where the oysters were left most completely alone.
It rose slowly and then, once the coat passed a certain thickness, began to slip, as the nacre kept building unevenly.
It fell, but those leases made up for it by producing fewer, far larger pearls that buyers prized for their size.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names the setup (the wholly-undisturbed leases) but not the direction -- the grade could in principle have kept rising, collapsed, or turned; the answer is the reported empirical shape. WHY-KEY (C): paragraph 3 states that in the leases 'that left the oysters wholly in peace, grade rose slowly and then, once the coat passed a certain thickness, began to slip, because the nacre went on building lump upon lopsided lump.' C paraphrases that rise-then-slip curve and its thickness hinge. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = quantifier / time overshoot -- pushes 'rose slowly' into an immediate collapse ('collapsed almost at once'), which the passage's 'rose slowly and then ... began to slip' plainly contradicts. B = surface_word_match / intuitive-false -- states the very belief the passage refutes: that grade climbs with thickness and peaks in the calmest leases; the text says it 'began to slip' once the coat passed a certain thickness, so this is the trap for a reader who imports the old rule. D = outside_knowledge -- adds a size trade-off ('fewer, far larger pearls prized for their size') the passage never reports; tempting because larger pearls sound valuable, but the text grades by roundness and evenness, not size.

questions.prompt: What does the text suggest is the reason a mild, regular disturbance produced rounder pearls than leaving the oysters wholly in peace?

options.text: Because the frequently turned panels kept the oysters in cooler, cleaner water that slowed their growth and refined the nacre they laid.
Because only constant handling can make a pearl round, and any oyster left in peace for long is bound to build a worthless, lumpy coat.
Because a disturbed oyster lays down less nacre, and a thinner coat is naturally rounder than a thick one.
Because roundness comes from evenly laid layers, and a gentle unsettling keeps the mantle seated straight, which uninterrupted growth alone does not.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), one logical inch beyond the text. STEM-LAW: the stem reports the finding (mild disturbance gave rounder pearls) and asks for the mechanism, which is not entailed by the finding. WHY-KEY (D): paragraph 3 states that a pearl's worth 'follows the evenness of its layers', and that 'Each gentle unsettling ... makes the oyster draw in and reseat its mantle squarely against the nucleus, so the layers that follow go on straight; left entirely undisturbed, the mantle drifts, and the coat thickens to one side.' The one-inch inference is that the disturbance helps because it keeps the mantle laying even layers -- exactly what D states, without overstating. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong mechanism / contradicted -- the study held 'the water temperature and the oyster strain steady', so the disturbed oysters did not enjoy cooler or cleaner water; tempting because cleaned panels sound as if they would improve conditions. B = quantifier_upgrade / too_far -- inflates the bounded finding into 'only constant handling can make a pearl round' and 'any oyster left in peace ... is bound to build a worthless coat', which contradicts the passage's own report that undisturbed grade 'rose slowly' first and that too much disturbance kills the oyster. C = too_literal -- restates that a disturbed oyster 'lays down less nacre' (true) but then equates thinness with roundness, whereas the passage ties roundness to the evenness of the layers, not to the coat being thin.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward Broek's rule that a working oyster should never be touched?

options.text: Broadly sympathetic: she largely shares Broek's rule and treats the gains in roundness as a minor effect that careful, patient farming would probably iron out.
Qualified: she grants that handling costs real growth and too much ruins the crop, yet holds a measured disturbance buys a roundness stillness cannot.
Convinced: she shares Broek's certainty that an oyster left wholly alone makes the better pearl, whatever a harvest is graded for.
Dismissive: she concludes that leaving oysters in peace is plainly bad practice and that the nacre calm oysters lay is worthless.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance / tone / attitude), edge position 5; signature trap is attribution swap. HEDGE-BALANCED by design: options A and B are both cautious in form ('Broadly sympathetic', 'Qualified'), so the item cannot be solved by picking the lone hedged option among absolutes -- the discriminator is direction and content. WHY-KEY (B): Broek states the absolute rule ('Never touch a working oyster ... Leave them be and the pearls take care of themselves'), but the writer answers in paragraph 5 that 'The six-year record, read whole, is more divided than Broek's rule allows', granting that 'the growth it costs is real' and that 'past the threshold the losses are steep', while insisting that 'a measured disturbance ... buys an evenness that perfect stillness never does'. 'Qualified' names exactly that real-but-bounded position. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = direction reversal, hedged in form (the working-shape trap) -- it sounds as cautious as the key but lands the writer on Broek's side, downgrading the roundness result to 'a minor effect ... careful farming would probably iron out'; the passage does the opposite, treating the roundness gain as the number that matters. C = role_or_attribution_swap (the signature TYPE-005 trap) -- assigns Broek's quoted certainty to the writer; tempting because his 'Never touch a working oyster' is the most emphatic stance in the passage. D = polarity overshoot -- pushes the writer's reservation into outright dismissal, calling the calm oysters' nacre 'worthless', which the concession that they 'laid down more nacre, and laid it faster' plainly contradicts.

fictional_entities: Renata Vasco, shellfish physiologist and study lead (invented; all findings invented)
Tomas Broek, owner of the bay's largest lease (invented; all quotes invented)
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge, pos1) / Q2 detail (nacre thickness rose in both arms alike) / Q3 detail (STEM-LAW: stem names the undisturbed leases, direction is the reported rise-then-slip curve) / Q4 inference (mantle-reseat / evenness mechanism, one inch) / Q5 stance (edge, pos5, HEDGE-BALANCED, attribution-swap)
generator_meta.key_letters: B, A, C, D, B -- all four letters used, no monotonic run and no positional tell; keys mix confident-specific (Q2 both-arms result, Q3 rise-then-slip curve, Q4 mechanism) with bounded/qualified (Q1 verdict, Q5 'qualified') so 'pick the hedged answer' does not score the unit blind
generator_meta.engineered_traps: quantifier_upgrade at Q2/B ('right across the bay' -> 'without any limit at all') and Q4/B ('a measured disturbance helps' -> 'only constant handling can make a pearl round'); cross-arm swap at Q2/C (thickness gain assigned only to the undisturbed leases) and Q3/B (thickness-peaks-in-calmest-leases, the refuted old rule); attribution_swap at Q5/C (Broek's absolute assigned to the writer)
generator_meta.length_tell_discipline: The key is the single longest option in NONE of the five questions (Q1 longest is A; Q2 longest is B; Q3 longest is B; Q4 longest is B; Q5 longest is A). option_length_ratio per question well under the 2.36 cap; verified with mech M-TELL (pass).
generator_meta.stance_item_fix: Q5 uses the batch-7-proven two-hedge shape. Both A ('Broadly sympathetic') and B (key, 'Qualified') are cautious in form, so no lone-hedge-among-absolutes structural leak survives; the item is decided by direction/content -- does the writer treat the roundness result as a minor fixable effect (A, siding with Broek) or as the number that matters (B, the passage's verdict). C and D are the absolutes (attribution-swap 'Convinced', polarity-overshoot 'Dismissive').
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q); this intentionally exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, which was authored for single-question seeded eval items. All other schema constraints pass as-is.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved all five from the passage alone before consulting keys: Q1=B, Q2=A, Q3=C, Q4=D, Q5=B (5/5 match). No item blind-solved to two defensible answers; the Q4 near-two-way (D vs C) closes because a thin coat is not automatically round -- the passage ties roundness to evenness of layers, not thinness -- and the Q5 two-hedge design requires the paragraph-5 'more divided than Broek's rule allows' verdict to separate B from A.
